title: CHAPTER THIRTY-THREE: TIMEPIECE
content: The Ashford Foundation doesn't look like itself tonight.

They've gutted the main hall and rebuilt it as a cathedral of industry — vaulted ceiling stripped to its original beams, industrial rig lights crisscrossing overhead like a constellation of sharp white stars. The runway is a vein of polished concrete cutting through two hundred black-tie guests, and their voices layer into a single electric hum that I can feel in the soles of my feet. Champagne corks pop somewhere in the distance, and the mist of it catches on my tongue — dry, bright, celebratory. The air backstage smells of chalk dust and silk and the faint metallic tang of garment racks, and I breathe it in like oxygen before a dive.
I'm wearing a simple black tunic of my own design. No embellishment. No statement. I'm not the spectacle tonight. The clothes are.

Behind me, the TIMEPIECE collection hangs on twelve racks — twelve garments spanning four centuries of living memory. My memory. The memory of bodies that wore and were worn, that dressed for coronations and funerals and revolutions and mornings that meant nothing to anyone but the soul inside the fabric. I can feel them behind me like a choir waiting for their downbeat.
My hands tremble. I press my palms flat against my thighs and will them still.

But the hum — the constant frequency that's lived inside my bones for weeks, the one that's driven me to sketch at three a.m. and rip seams and rebuild and doubt and rebuild again — the hum is quiet. The quietest it's been in weeks. Like even my body knows: this is the moment. This is what all that noise was for.
The house lights dim. A single spotlight hits the head of the runway.
The show begins.
THE CONVERSATION walks first.

The 1620s doublet — slashed sleeves, padded shoulder, the architecture of a man who wanted the world to know his status before he opened his mouth — fused with a modern bomber jacket. Wool and leather. Century upon century. The model's heels strike the polished concrete in a rhythm that syncs with the house music — low, pulsing, electronic — and the sound is precise and military and old. The audience gasps. I can hear it — a collective intake, two hundred lungs seizing at once. They don't know what they're looking at. They just know it's never been done before.

THE MASK walks second — Venetian carnival meets modern couture. A face obscured by beaded lace, the body wrapped in velvet that shifts color under the lights like an oil slick on canal water. The guests lean forward in their seats. They want to see behind the mask. They always do. They don't understand that the mask *is* the point — that every century has worn one, that the face beneath was never the secret.
THE MOURNING walks third.

Midnight blue silk, heavy as a wound, with a raw hem that looks like it was torn rather than cut. The fabric pools at the model's feet like water collecting in a doorway. The room goes silent. Not gasping-silent. *Church*-silent. The kind of silence that happens when people recognize something they can't name — a grief that isn't theirs but feels like it could have been. That dress has lived through more funerals than anyone in this room will ever attend. And they feel it. They won't say "I felt someone else's sorrow in that dress." They'll say it was beautiful. They'll say it was haunting.
They won't know how right they are.

Piece by piece, the models walk. THE REVOLUTION — 1789 redingote deconstructed into asymmetric wool and brass buttons — draws murmurs of recognition. THE INDUSTRY — Victorian tailoring reimagined as sharpedged power suiting, soot-stained lapels that aren't stained at all but woven that way, dark threads running through charcoal wool like smoke through a factory window — makes a woman in the third row clutch her companion's arm.

I watch from the wings. The concrete is cool and smooth under my bare soles — I kicked off my shoes ten minutes ago, needed the ground, needed something real. The models pass me on their way back, and each one gives me a look — not gratitude, not exactly, but *recognition*. They felt it too. Each garment carries its century like a heartbeat, and the women wearing them know something shifted when they put them on.
Rowan is in the front row.

He's not watching the clothes. He's watching me — my face in the wings, half-lit by the spill of the spotlight. And his expression is the one I've seen on other faces in other centuries, the one that reaches across time like a hand through water: love that can't look away. Love that won't. I've seen that look in a cottage in Dorset and a flat in Bloomsbury and a hospital room in Queens, and it hasn't changed in four hundred years. It doesn't change. He doesn't change. And the hum, already quiet, dips lower still — a frequency so calm it's almost silence, and I think: *this is what peace feels like, this is what it's supposed to sound like.*
Maren Voss is three rows back.

She's wearing white. Of course she is. A suit, immaculate, clinical, the kind of white that suggests nothing has ever touched her. Her face is an unreadable mask — no expression, no reaction to the clothes, no gasping, no leaning forward. She might be watching a spreadsheet. She might be watching a body being lowered into the ground. It's the same face either way.
The collection is winning. The audience is captivated. Everything is working.

THE PEARL walks — the 1920s, dropped waist and beaded fringe, but the beads are seed pearls, and the dress moves like moonlight on water, and someone in the back actually *sighs*. THE UTILITY is next — the wartime coat, 1940s, restructured from military wool with red cross stitching visible at the collar, practical and devastating. The model's steps are slower for this one. She didn't rehearse it that way. The coat told her how to walk.
And then Dimitri Valk walks in.

He wasn't invited. I know because I approved the guest list myself — every name, every seat, every placement vetted for exactly what it could give the collection. Dimitri Valk was not on that list. He moves through the standing-room section at the back with the ease of someone who belongs everywhere and nowhere, and he's not subtle. He's carrying a leather portfolio — brown, worn at the corners, the kind that holds documents, the kind that holds *evidence*.
He takes the empty seat beside Maren Voss.
They exchange a look. Brief. Professional. *Complicit.* Like two surgeons confirming the organ is prepped. Like two generals acknowledging the flank is exposed.

Rowan sees them together. I watch his jaw tighten from across the room — the muscle jumping beneath his skin, his hands gripping the armrests of his chair. He knows. He doesn't know what's in the portfolio, but he knows what Maren is, and he knows what Dimitri does, and he knows the two of them side by side is not coincidence.
My blood goes cold.

Not the hum. Not the frequency. Something older — something pre-verbal, something that lived in the body before the mind had language for it. The animal knowing that the shadow at the edge of the firelight has teeth.

The show continues. It has to continue. The models are walking. The audience is watching. Eight looks down, four to go, and I cannot stop the machinery of this thing I built because a man with a portfolio sat down beside a woman in white.

THE REBEL walks ninth. Punk meets Victorian — safety pins through lace, a bustle constructed from distressed leather, the anarchy of one century wearing the corset of another. The audience roars. They *love* this one. It's the most accessible — the one that looks like fashion, like rebellion, like something they can understand without having to feel it first.

THE SIGNAL walks tenth. Techwear meets corsetry — matte black performance fabric boned with flexible steel, LED thread running through the seams like circuitry, like nerves. The lights in the room dim further and the dress *glows*, and I hear someone say something I can't quite make out, and it doesn't matter because the sound in this room right now is the sound of two hundred people experiencing something they've never experienced before.
THE ECHO walks last.

The twelfth look. The gown that references every century at once. A column of ivory silk with the silhouette of the 1620s at the shoulder, the waistline of the 1780s, the hemline of the 1920s, the deconstruction of the 1940s, the rebellion of the 1970s, the technology of now. Every seam is a suture. Every layer is a life. The model walks slowly — she's been instructed to walk slowly, and for once she follows the instruction — and the audience rises.

They're on their feet before she reaches the end of the runway. The applause is thunderous — not polite, not perfunctory, but *volcanic*. The sound of two hundred people who have been cracked open and don't know how to close themselves again. The house music swells. The lights come up. People are crying. A man in the fifth row has his hand over his mouth. The woman beside Maren is dabbing her eyes with a cocktail napkin.
It's a triumph.
I step out from the wings.

The spotlight finds me. The applause doesn't stop — if anything, it grows, cresting into something that sounds like the inside of a wave. I make my way down the runway, the polished concrete cool and absolute beneath my feet, and I can feel every century in my body bowing at once. The hum is silent now — not suppressed, not silenced, but *fulfilled*. Like a question that has finally been answered. Like a clock that has finished striking.
I take my bow.
And as I straighten, my eyes find Dimitri Valk.

He's not clapping. He's holding up his phone — not recording the show, not filming the moment, not doing any of the things that two hundred other phones in this room are doing right now. He's showing the screen to the person beside him. Tilting it. Displaying.
On the screen: a document. Official layout. Letterhead I recognize because I've received letters on it — grants, congratulations, institutional courtesies.
Ashford Foundation letterhead.
Medical records.
*My* medical records.

The kind of records that exist in databases with access logs. The kind that someone with institutional authority — someone like a foundation director, someone like Maren Voss — could pull. The kind that contain information I've never shared, histories written in clinical language that reduce four centuries of living memory to a diagnosis, an anomaly, a set of data points that make no medical sense and will raise every question I've spent lifetimes avoiding.
Maren got access.

The applause is still thundering. Two hundred people on their feet, tears drying on their faces, calling my name. The spotlight is warm on my skin. The collection — every piece, every century, every stitch that cost me sleep and sanity and weeks of a hum that wouldn't stop — the collection is a triumph. It's *real*. It's the truest thing I've ever made.

And Dimitri Valk is sitting in the third row with my medical records on his phone, and he doesn't need to stand up, doesn't need to shout, doesn't need to interrupt a single moment of this triumph. He just needs the show to end. He just needs the applause to stop. He just needs the audience to leave and the reviews to land and the world to know my name — because the world knowing my name is exactly what makes the exposure devastating. You can't disappear from a spotlight. You can't vanish from a stage.

Rowan is staring at me from the front row, and his expression has shifted — love that can't look away becoming something sharper, something ready, something that looks like the man who once stood in a burning building and refused to leave without me. He sees my face. He knows.
Maren Voss hasn't moved. Her lips curve — the smallest, most precise expression I have ever seen on a human face. Not a smile. A confirmation.

The applause crashes around me like ocean, like standing under a waterfall of sound, and I am frozen in the spotlight with the concrete cool beneath my feet and the champagne mist still bright on my tongue, and I am thinking: *this is what it sounds like when everything you've built is perfect and everything you've hidden is about to be taken.*
Dimitri meets my eyes across the room. He doesn't smile either. He doesn't need to.
The silence of his approach is louder than the applause.
